title: The young woman with episodic pounding headaches and a neck mass
blurb: A TCA enzyme that doubles as a tumor suppressor — when it fails, a catecholamine-secreting paraganglioma grows and succinate becomes an oncometabolite.

patient.complaint: Episodes of pounding headache, palpitations, and drenching sweats; a painless lump in the neck.

opening: 29-year-old woman with months of paroxysmal pounding headaches, palpitations, sweating, and pallor, plus blood pressures spiking to 190/110 during episodes. She has a firm, pulsatile painless mass at the carotid bifurcation. Plasma free metanephrines are elevated and CT shows an avidly enhancing skull-base/neck lesion. Her father died of a 'neck tumor.' What do you want to ask, examine, or order next?
history: Paroxysmal adrenergic spells for ~1 year. Family history: father and paternal aunt had head-and-neck paragangliomas (autosomal dominant, parent-of-origin pattern). No diabetes. No medication triggers.
exam: During a spell: HR 124, BP 188/108, diaphoretic, pale. Firm pulsatile mass at the left carotid bifurcation (carotid body), non-tender, mobile side-to-side but not vertically. No thyromegaly. No abdominal mass.

prompts.diagnosis: What is the diagnosis (tumor + the gene family)?
prompts.mechanism: Briefly: how does losing a TCA enzyme cause a TUMOR (the oncometabolite link)?

pinpoint.prompt: Click the TCA enzyme that is lost in this tumor (it is also ETC Complex II).
pinpoint.correctMsg: Yes — succinate dehydrogenase (Complex II). Loss of SDH blocks succinate → fumarate, so succinate accumulates. Succinate is an ONCOMETABOLITE: it inhibits α-KG-dependent prolyl hydroxylases, stabilizing HIF-1α → a pseudohypoxic, pro-angiogenic state that drives paraganglioma/pheochromocytoma.
pinpoint.wrongMsg: Not quite. The enzyme is the only one embedded in the inner mitochondrial membrane — it's both a TCA enzyme AND ETC Complex II.
pinpoint.revealMsg: Target: SDH (CII) (Succinate → Fumarate). SDHx mutations (SDHB/C/D) cause hereditary paraganglioma-pheochromocytoma; SDHB carries the highest malignant/metastatic risk.

mcqs.stem: How does accumulated succinate promote tumorigenesis?
choices: It inhibits α-ketoglutarate-dependent dioxygenases (prolyl hydroxylases, TET/histone demethylases), stabilizing HIF-1α and altering the epigenome
mcqs.explanation: Succinate (like fumarate and 2-HG) competitively inhibits α-KG-dependent enzymes. Blocking prolyl hydroxylases stabilizes HIF-1α (pseudohypoxia → VEGF, angiogenesis); blocking TET/JmjC demethylases produces a hypermethylator phenotype. Hence 'oncometabolite.'

mcqs.stem: Before any surgery or biopsy of a catecholamine-secreting paraganglioma, what is essential?
choices: Alpha-blockade first (e.g., phenoxybenzamine), then beta-blockade, to prevent hypertensive crisis
mcqs.explanation: Unopposed alpha stimulation can precipitate a hypertensive crisis. Always alpha-block FIRST (phenoxybenzamine/doxazosin) with volume expansion, then add beta-blockade for tachycardia. Never beta-block first.

mcqs.stem: Which other TCA-linked oncometabolite syndrome parallels this mechanism?
choices: Fumarate hydratase (FH) loss → hereditary leiomyomatosis and renal cell cancer; and IDH1/2 mutations → 2-hydroxyglutarate in glioma/AML
mcqs.explanation: FH loss accumulates fumarate (HLRCC), and mutant IDH produces 2-HG (gliomas, AML). All three — succinate, fumarate, 2-HG — are α-KG-mimicking oncometabolites that dysregulate the same dioxygenases.
